**SETTLEMENT, LICENSE, AND RELEASE AGREEMENT**

This Settlement, License, and Release Agreement (the “Agreement”) is entered into as of the date of the last signature below (the “Effective Date”), by and between:

**Company**: the Virginia biotechnology company described in the recitals below (“Company”); and

**Friend**: the individual public figure described in the recitals below (“Friend”).

Company and Friend are referred to individually as a “Party” and collectively as the “Parties.”

**RECITALS**

A. Company develops and owns proprietary low-THC hemp seed genetics used in producing varieties grown for extraction into concentrates for wellness and industrial products.

B. Approximately two years prior to the Effective Date, Company’s founder began a personal relationship with Friend, who is active in the cannabis industry and had expressed interest in growing industrial hemp.

C. Several months into the relationship, the Parties separated, and hemp plants containing Company’s proprietary genetics (the “Genetics”) disappeared from an off-site experimental greenhouse located in Virginia (the “Greenhouse”) known only to Company’s founder and Friend.

D. Approximately six months later, Friend began publicly promoting a low-THC variety (the “New Genetics”) that exhibits multiple traits identical to those of the missing plants.

E. Following informal communications, Company asserted that Friend was commercializing the strain and distributing derivative seed stock. Friend denied any infringement and asserted that the underlying genome originated from Friend’s own parental lines, with no agreements existing between the Parties.

F. Both Parties desire to resolve the dispute privately, without litigation, while preserving the option for future collaboration. Friend has proposed, and Company has agreed in principle to, a lump-sum payment plus continuing royalties tied to net seed sales for a limited period, subject to the protections set forth herein.

G. Company requires that any release exclude regulatory or tax penalties and that Company retain audit rights over Friend’s production and sales data. The Parties intend this Agreement to preserve Company’s commercial interests, clearly define each Party’s continuing rights to the disputed material, and ensure that any future use or disclosure occurs under enforceable terms, while complying with applicable Virginia law, including Va. Code § 3.2-4115(A).

NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth below, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows:

**1. DEFINITIONS**

1.1 “Genetics” means Company’s proprietary low-THC hemp seed genetics and any derivatives or progeny thereof.

1.2 “New Genetics” means the low-THC hemp variety promoted by Friend and any seed stock, plants, or derivative products derived therefrom or exhibiting the traits at issue in the dispute.

1.3 “Net Sales” means the gross invoiced amounts received by Friend (or its affiliates or licensees) from sales or licenses of seeds, plants, or derivative products incorporating the New Genetics, less documented returns, allowances, discounts, and sales or value-added taxes actually paid.

1.4 “Term” means the period commencing on the Effective Date and continuing for five (5) years thereafter, unless earlier terminated as provided herein.

**2. SETTLEMENT PAYMENT**

2.1 Within ten (10) business days of the Effective Date, Friend shall pay Company a one-time lump-sum settlement payment of Seventy-Five Thousand Dollars ($75,000) (the “Lump Sum Payment”) by wire transfer to an account designated by Company.

**3. ROYALTIES**

3.1 During the Term, Friend shall pay Company a royalty equal to three percent (3%) of Net Sales of any seeds, plants, or derivative products incorporating the New Genetics.

3.2 Royalties shall be calculated and paid quarterly within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter. Each payment shall be accompanied by a written statement detailing the calculation of Net Sales for the quarter.

3.3 All royalty payments are non-refundable and shall survive any termination of this Agreement with respect to Net Sales occurring during the Term.

**4. LICENSE GRANT AND RIGHTS**

4.1 Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Company grants Friend a non-exclusive, non-transferable, non-sublicensable license during the Term to use, grow, handle, process, and commercially distribute the New Genetics solely within the Commonwealth of Virginia.

4.2 The license is limited to activities that comply with all applicable federal and state laws governing industrial hemp. Friend may not assign, transfer, or encumber the license without Company’s prior written consent.

4.3 Company retains all right, title, and interest in and to the Genetics, including all intellectual property rights, trade secrets, and proprietary information. Nothing in this Agreement constitutes an admission by Friend of any infringement or misappropriation.

**5. AUDIT RIGHTS**

5.1 Company shall have the right, upon at least ten (10) business days’ prior written notice, to conduct an audit of Friend’s relevant books, records, production data, sales reports, and inventory records relating to the New Genetics to verify Net Sales and compliance with this Agreement. Audits shall occur during normal business hours and no more than once per calendar year.

5.2 Friend shall cooperate fully and make all requested materials available. If an audit reveals an underpayment of more than five percent (5%), Friend shall bear the reasonable costs of the audit and promptly remit any shortfall plus interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month.

**6. REGULATORY AND LEGAL COMPLIANCE**

6.1 Friend represents, warrants, and covenants that Friend shall, at Friend’s sole expense, obtain and maintain all registrations, licenses, and authorizations required to grow, handle, process, or distribute industrial hemp in Virginia, including registration with the Commissioner pursuant to Va. Code § 3.2-4115(A) and any successor or related provisions.

6.2 Friend shall ensure that all activities involving the New Genetics comply with the registration requirements, inspection consents, location specifications, and other conditions set forth in Va. Code § 3.2-4115 and applicable federal regulations (including 7 C.F.R. Part 990).

6.3 Friend shall not engage in any activity that would expose Company to regulatory, administrative, or governmental liability. Company shall have no obligation to assist Friend with any registration, application, inspection, or compliance matter.

**7. RELEASE**

7.1 Except for the obligations expressly created by this Agreement, Friend hereby releases, acquits, and forever discharges Company and its officers, directors, employees, agents, successors, and assigns from any and all claims, demands, damages, actions, causes of action, liabilities, or controversies arising out of or related to the missing Genetics, the New Genetics, the Greenhouse, or the dispute described in the Recitals, whether known or unknown, up to the Effective Date.

7.2 Except for the obligations expressly created by this Agreement, Company hereby releases, acquits, and forever discharges Friend from any and all claims, demands, damages, actions, causes of action, liabilities, or controversies arising out of or related to the dispute described in the Recitals, whether known or unknown, up to the Effective Date.

7.3 The releases in Sections 7.1 and 7.2 do not extend to, and expressly exclude, any claims, liabilities, penalties, fines, assessments, or obligations arising from or related to regulatory, governmental, tax, or administrative matters, including but not limited to any penalties or liabilities under Va. Code § 3.2-4115 or other hemp-related statutes, tax reporting, or criminal or civil enforcement actions by any governmental authority.

7.4 This release is made without any admission of liability or wrongdoing by either Party.

**8. INDEMNIFICATION**

8.1 Friend shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Company and its officers, directors, employees, agents, successors, and assigns from and against any and all claims, demands, damages, losses, liabilities, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys’ fees) arising out of or related to: (a) Friend’s use, growth, handling, or distribution of the New Genetics; (b) any breach of this Agreement by Friend; or (c) Friend’s failure to comply with applicable laws or obtain required registrations, except to the extent such claim arises solely from Company’s gross negligence or willful misconduct.

**9. CONFIDENTIALITY**

9.1 The Parties agree to keep confidential the terms of this Agreement and any proprietary or non-public information exchanged in connection with the dispute or this settlement. This obligation shall survive termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years.

**10. NO FUTURE OBLIGATIONS**

10.1 This Agreement does not create any obligation for future collaboration, but it expressly preserves each Party’s right to negotiate separate agreements in the future on mutually acceptable terms.

**11. GOVERNING LAW AND DISPUTE RESOLUTION**

11.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia, without regard to conflicts of law principles.

11.2 Any dispute arising under this Agreement shall first be submitted to good-faith mediation in Richmond, Virginia. If mediation does not resolve the dispute within thirty (30) days, either Party may pursue binding arbitration administered by JAMS in Richmond, Virginia, under its Comprehensive Arbitration Rules. The arbitration shall be confidential, and judgment upon the award may be entered in any court of competent jurisdiction.

**12. MISCELLANEOUS**

12.1 This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties concerning the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, or agreements.

12.2 No amendment or waiver shall be effective unless in writing and signed by both Parties.

12.3 If any provision is held invalid or unenforceable, the remaining provisions shall continue in full force and effect.

12.4 This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original.

12.5 Neither Party may assign this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other, except that Company may assign to an affiliate or successor.

12.6 All notices shall be in writing and delivered by email with read receipt, overnight courier, or certified mail to the addresses provided by the Parties.

12.7 This Agreement shall bind and inure to the benefit of the Parties and their respective successors and permitted assigns.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Parties have executed this Agreement as of the Effective Date.

**LEGAL MEMORANDUM**

**To:** [Supervising Attorney]  
**From:** [AI Legal Assistant]  
**Date:** [Current Date]  
**Re:** Potential Abduction Charges Against Jim Under Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A) – Analysis re: Victim Bobby and Bank Tellers (Janet, Drive-Through Teller, and Others) in Wells Fargo Bank Robbery, Richmond, Virginia (May 23, 2025)

**Question Presented**  
Does the evidence support separate charges of abduction under Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A) against Jim for his conduct toward customer Bobby and the bank tellers (including Janet and the drive-through teller), independent of the robbery charges? What are the statutory elements, applicable case law, and likelihood of conviction on each?

**Brief Answer**  
Yes, viable abduction charges exist under Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A), particularly for Bobby (high likelihood of conviction) and possibly for the tellers (moderate likelihood, depending on whether detention is deemed incidental to robbery). The statute requires proof of (1) force, intimidation, or deception without legal justification; (2) seizure, taking, transport, detention, or secreting of the victim; and (3) intent to deprive the victim of personal liberty. Virginia case law (e.g., *Brown v. Commonwealth*, *Hoyt v. Commonwealth*, *Cardwell v. Commonwealth*) requires the detention to be “separate and apart from, and not merely incidental to,” the restraint inherent in the underlying robbery. The facts here—gunpoint orders to lie on the floor, physical striking of Bobby, and continued detention during/after money collection—support separate charges. Conviction is likely for Bobby (distinct physical force and wallet/watch theft while detained) but closer for tellers (brief post-money orders to lie down may be viewed as incidental per *Hoyt*).

**Statement of Facts**  
On May 23, 2025, at approximately 3:40 p.m., Jim entered the Wells Fargo Bank in Richmond, Virginia, wearing a mask (covering nose and mouth), gray hoodie, and yellow vest, and carrying a black handgun. He pointed the gun at employees and customers, demanded money be placed in a bag he carried, and used the gun to “direct” tellers in a “forceful” and “demanding” manner.  

- **Bobby (customer):** While speaking to a teller, Bobby heard Jim’s voice, turned, and saw the masked man in the yellow vest. Jim struck Bobby across the face with the gun in his right hand, ordered him to lie on the floor, and took Bobby’s wallet and watch. Bobby complied out of fear (“I threw myself to the ground because I saw that person with a weapon and I was fearful, I felt threatened”). He remained on the floor, head down, until Jim left, stating he stayed because he was “scared and there was an armed man there and he did not know what was going to happen.”  
- **Bank Tellers:** Janet ducked behind her desk upon hearing a scream and seeing the armed, masked man. After placing money in the bag, Jim commanded her to lie face down on the floor. The drive-through teller heard screams, observed the man with the black bag and small pistol, and saw him use the gun to direct tellers to fill the bag. The service manager screamed upon seeing the armed man. Jim went straight to the teller line after robbing Bobby.  

These facts mirror the underlying conduct in *Walker v. Commonwealth*, 74 Va. App. 475 (2023) (aff’d on related issues), where similar bank robbery facts led to an abduction conviction.

**Discussion**  

**I. Statutory Elements of Abduction (Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A))**  
Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A) defines abduction as follows:  
“Any person who, by force, intimidation or deception, and without legal justification or excuse, seizes, takes, transports, detains or secretes another person with the intent to deprive such other person of his personal liberty or to withhold or conceal him from any person, authority or institution lawfully entitled to his charge, shall be deemed guilty of ‘abduction.’”  

The terms “abduction” and “kidnapping” are synonymous. Punishment is generally a Class 5 felony (absent other enhancements). Subsection (C) excludes law-enforcement officers acting in duty.  

Key elements to prove beyond a reasonable doubt:  
1. **Force, intimidation, or deception** – without legal justification or excuse.  
2. **Seizure/taking/transport/detention/secreting** of the victim.  
3. **Specific intent** to deprive the victim of personal liberty (or to withhold/conceal from lawful authority).  

Mere incidental restraint during another crime (e.g., robbery) does not support a separate abduction conviction. *Brown v. Commonwealth*, 230 Va. 310, 314 (1985) (abduction must be “separate and apart from, and not merely incidental to, the restraint employed in the commission of the other crime”).  

**II. Application to Bobby**  
The evidence strongly supports an abduction charge separate from robbery.  

- **Force/Intimidation**: Jim used a handgun to strike Bobby across the face and pointed it at him. Bobby explicitly stated he lay down and remained because he felt threatened by the weapon.  
- **Detention**: Jim ordered Bobby to the floor; Bobby remained there (head down) throughout the remainder of the robbery and until Jim’s departure. This was not fleeting.  
- **Intent to Deprive of Liberty**: The detention facilitated the theft of Bobby’s wallet and watch while Bobby was immobilized and fearful. Bobby remained on the floor precisely because of the ongoing armed threat.  

This detention exceeds the minimal restraint inherent in a robbery. Unlike *Hoyt v. Commonwealth*, 44 Va. App. 489 (2004) (victim forced ~10 feet to register during ~5-minute robbery; held incidental), here Jim physically assaulted Bobby, robbed him personally while on the floor, and left him detained. *Cardwell v. Commonwealth*, 248 Va. 501 (1994) (transporting victim after robbery to avoid detection and retain fruits supported separate abduction). The facts align closely with the abduction conviction upheld in the underlying *Walker* scenario.  

**Likelihood of Conviction**: High. The physical striking, specific order to the floor, and continued immobilization while robbing Bobby distinguish this from incidental restraint. A jury could readily find the detention was for pecuniary benefit (retaining control over Bobby to complete the personal robbery and escape).  

**III. Application to the Bank Tellers (Janet, Drive-Through Teller, Others)**  
Viable but closer call; conviction possible but more susceptible to an “incidental” defense.  

- **Force/Intimidation**: Jim brandished a handgun, pointed it at tellers, used it to “direct” them to fill the bag, and spoke in a “forceful” and “demanding” manner. Janet ducked upon seeing the armed man; the drive-through teller observed the gun used to control movement.  
- **Detention**: After money was placed in the bag, Jim specifically commanded Janet to lie face down. Tellers were ordered to comply under gunpoint. The service manager screamed and was present during the takeover.  
- **Intent to Deprive of Liberty**: The orders to the floor occurred during/after money collection and appear aimed at preventing resistance or interference while Jim completed the robbery and exited.  

Per *Hoyt*, short-duration detention (here, likely minutes) and minimal asportation (e.g., ducking behind desks or moving to lie down) during an ongoing robbery may be viewed as inherent to the crime. However, the explicit post-money command to lie face down (Janet) and the use of the gun to “direct” compliance go beyond simple taking of property. *Brown* and *Cardwell* emphasize that detention to facilitate escape or retain control can support a separate charge. The *Walker* facts (identical bank setting, yellow vest, gunpoint demands, customer struck and ordered down) resulted in an abduction conviction at trial.  

**Likelihood of Conviction**: Moderate to high for tellers like Janet (explicit floor order after compliance). Lower for brief or less individualized interactions (e.g., drive-through teller observing from afar). The Commonwealth can argue the floor orders were not necessary for the robbery itself but served to immobilize witnesses during escape. If the court applies a strict *Hoyt* analysis, the charge could be stricken as incidental; however, the multi-victim, prolonged gunpoint control in a bank setting strengthens the prosecution position.

**IV. Additional Considerations and Defenses**  
- **Merger/ Double Jeopardy**: Virginia follows the *Brown* rule to avoid punishing the same restraint twice. The memorandum should note that abduction is charged separately only when the detention is distinct.  
- **Use of Firearm**: Any abduction conviction would likely pair with a firearm enhancement under Va. Code § 18.2-53.1 (second/subsequent offense rules may apply if prior convictions exist, per *Walker*).  
- **Defenses**: Jim may argue all restraints were inherent in the robbery (citing *Hoyt*). However, the physical assault on Bobby and specific post-compliance floor orders weaken this.  
- **Sentencing**: Abduction is a Class 5 felony (1–10 years); can be enhanced if for pecuniary benefit or with firearm.  

**Conclusion**  
The evidence supports abduction charges under Va. Code Ann. § 18.2-47(A) for both Bobby and the tellers. For Bobby, the distinct physical force, personal robbery while detained, and continued immobilization make conviction highly likely. For the tellers, the gunpoint directives and explicit orders to lie down after money collection provide a reasonable basis for a separate charge, though success depends on proving the detention was not merely incidental (*Hoyt* distinction). The facts closely track the *Walker* prosecution, where abduction was successfully pursued. I recommend charging abduction for pecuniary benefit for Bobby (strongest case) and for key tellers such as Janet to maximize leverage.  

Please let me know if you require a draft indictment, jury instructions, or further research on related offenses (robbery, firearm use).

**Subject:** Advisory Regarding Likely Review Board Decision on Reported Violations by Spanish Vessel Under the Convention on Conduct of Fishing Operations in the North Atlantic

Dear Spanish Competent Authorities,

I am writing to provide an advisory assessment, based on the Convention on Conduct of Fishing Operations in the North Atlantic (London, 1 June 1967, as rectified), of the likely outcome before the review board in the matter reported by Belgian authorised officers. The facts as described involve (i) observation of unmarked anchored lines in the sea and (ii) the subsequent approach by Belgian authorised officers to a Spanish-flagged vessel to seek information, which was refused. The Belgian officers have reported the incident and argued that the refusal constitutes an additional violation of Spain’s obligations.

**Relevant Convention Provisions**

The primary infringement arises under **Article 5**, which requires that “Nets, lines and other gear anchored in the sea … shall be marked in order to indicate their position and extent” in accordance with **Annex IV, Rule 1**. This rule mandates flag or radar-reflector buoys by day and light buoys by night, with specific configurations for ends, extensions beyond 1 mile, and visibility standards. Failure to mark anchored lines is a clear breach of the Convention’s marking regime, which Contracting Parties (including Spain) are bound to enforce.

The approach by the Belgian officers falls under **Article 9**. Belgium and Spain are both Contracting Parties, and Spain’s reservation to paragraphs (5) and (6) of Article 9 (made under Article 14(3)) applies only vis-à-vis States that have themselves made a corresponding reservation. Belgium has not made such a reservation. Consequently, paragraphs (5) and (6) remain fully binding on Spain in its relations with Belgium.

Under **Article 9(5)**, an authorised officer who has reason to believe a vessel is not complying with the Convention (here, the unmarked lines) may “identify the vessel, seek to obtain the necessary information from the vessel and report.” The Belgian officers’ actions—identifying the vessel and approaching to request information—are squarely within this authority. **Article 9(10)** further provides that “Resistance by a vessel to the directions of an authorised officer shall be deemed as resistance to the authority of the flag State of that vessel.” Refusal to cooperate when information is sought in connection with a suspected infringement can reasonably be characterised as such resistance, particularly where the officers were acting to verify compliance.

**Article 8(1)** obliges each Contracting Party to “take such measures as may be appropriate to implement and enforce the provisions of this Convention with respect to its vessels and gear.” **Article 9(11)** requires Contracting Parties to “consider and act on reports of foreign authorised officers under these arrangements on the same basis as reports of national officers.”

**Likely Decision of the Review Board**

The review board (or other appropriate authority) is expected to conclude as follows:

1. **Primary Violation – Marking of Gear**: The Spanish vessel (or its gear) failed to comply with the mandatory marking requirements for anchored lines under Article 5 and Annex IV. This constitutes an infringement of the Convention.

2. **Secondary Issue – Non-Cooperation**: The refusal by the Spanish officers to provide the requested information when approached by duly authorised Belgian officers constitutes resistance under Article 9(10). This is deemed equivalent to resistance to Spanish authority and triggers Spain’s enforcement obligations under Article 8. While the Convention does not impose an explicit duty on vessels to volunteer information absent an order to stop or board, the officers’ authority to “seek to obtain the necessary information” (Article 9(5)) makes sustained refusal actionable, especially when the underlying marking violation provided reasonable grounds for enquiry.

3. **No Applicability of Damage-Specific Procedures**: Article 7’s review-board mechanism is limited to disputes concerning damaged gear or entanglement. The present matter concerns an infringement report rather than a damage claim; however, the board is likely to apply analogous principles of factual examination and cooperative resolution.

4. **Enforcement Recommendation**: The board will probably direct Spain to:
   - Investigate the incident and the conduct of the vessel’s master and crew.
   - Take appropriate enforcement action (e.g., administrative penalties, warnings, or other measures consistent with Spanish law) for both the marking violation and the resistance to the foreign authorised officer.
   - Report back to the Belgian authorities on the outcome, in accordance with the spirit of Article 9(11) and the general duty of Contracting Parties to collaborate.

**Recommendations for Spain**

- Spain should treat the Belgian report with the same weight as a domestic report and initiate prompt internal proceedings.
- Any defence based on the reservation to Article 9(5)–(6) is unlikely to succeed against Belgium.
- To avoid escalation, Spain may wish to demonstrate good-faith enforcement, which could include issuing guidance to Spanish fishing vessels on mandatory cooperation with authorised officers of other Contracting Parties and on strict adherence to Annex IV marking rules.

Please let me know if you require a more detailed analysis of possible sanctions, draft language for internal instructions, or assistance preparing a response to the Belgian authorities. I remain at your disposal.

Best regards,  
[Your Name]  
Legal Adviser, International Fisheries Law

**Subject:** Update on Lily Strum SSDI Appeal – Five-Step Analysis and Disability Determination

Dear Supervising Partner,

I am writing to summarize my review of Lily Strum’s case file and the new Medical Opinion Statement from Dr. Michael Jenkins (dated around late 2024), which has not yet been considered by the SSA. Ms. Strum’s claim was denied at both the initial and reconsideration levels, with the agency finding severe medically determinable impairments but limiting her to light work and concluding she was not disabled. She is now at the appeal stage. I have applied the five-step sequential evaluation process to the evidence in the attached documents, including her work history, prior SSA determinations, and the new medical opinion. My analysis follows.

**Step 1: Is the claimant engaged in substantial gainful activity?**  
No. Ms. Strum’s earnings record shows full-time teaching income through 2023 ($48,000 that year, following $66,000–$72,000 in prior years). There are no earnings reported for 2024 or 2025. She has not performed SGA since at least the end of 2023.

**Step 2: Does the claimant have a severe medically determinable impairment?**  
Yes. Dr. Jenkins diagnoses Crohn’s disease (confirmed by colonoscopy and biopsies on 09/08/2023) and uveitis in both eyes (confirmed by dilated eye exam on 10/11/2023). These impairments are severe because they cause significant limitations in basic work activities, as documented by multiple hospitalizations (7 days in December 2023 with hemoglobin 10.0 g/dL and BMI 18.0; 4 days in April 2024 with hemoglobin 9.9 g/dL, albumin 3.0 g/dL, BMI 17.4; 5 days in October 2024 with hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, albumin 3.3 g/dL, BMI 17.1), ongoing symptoms (fatigue, severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, frequent urgent bowel movements, rectal bleeding, eye pain), and physical findings (diffuse abdominal tenderness, right lower quadrant pain, weight loss, malnutrition, pallor, low-grade fever, irregularly shaped pupils, white blood cells in the anterior chamber). The opinion states these have a major effect on her ability to perform basic work activities.

**Step 3: Does the impairment(s) meet or equal a listed impairment?**  
This step warrants close examination. Ms. Strum’s Crohn’s disease may meet or medically equal Listing 5.06 (Inflammatory Bowel Disease). The record shows repeated hospitalizations for complications, anemia with hemoglobin levels consistently at or below 10.2 g/dL, low serum albumin (3.0–3.3 g/dL), persistently low BMI (17.1–18.0), significant weight loss, and abdominal tenderness. These align with the listing’s criteria involving obstruction, malnutrition, or persistent disease activity despite treatment (daily Lialda, Canasa, and corticosteroid eye drops). The uveitis adds visual and pain-related complications. If the ALJ finds the listing met, Ms. Strum would be disabled at this step without needing to proceed further. Even if not exactly met, the combination of impairments could equal a listing given the objective evidence and Dr. Jenkins’ opinion that limitations are consistent with the medical findings and are not expected to improve.

**Step 4: Can the claimant perform past relevant work?**  
No. Ms. Strum’s past relevant work was as a high school math teacher (skilled, no transferable skills, performed at a level consistent with light work per prior SSA findings). The new medical evidence indicates she cannot sustain the demands of that work. While Dr. Jenkins notes she can stand, walk, or sit for a full 8-hour day and lift up to 25 pounds, she experiences severe abdominal pain that interferes with basic work activities for 10% of the workday, requires lying down once for 15 minutes during an 8-hour period, and has eye pain from uveitis. These limitations, combined with fatigue, gastrointestinal symptoms, and recurrent hospitalizations, would prevent reliable performance of her prior teaching duties.

**Step 5: Can the claimant perform other work in the national economy?**  
Considering her age (born 10/10/1988, now in her mid-30s), high school education, and prior skilled work with no transferable skills, this step depends on her residual functional capacity. The prior SSA determinations limited her to light work and found other jobs existed. However, the new evidence from Dr. Jenkins—unreviewed by the SSA—strengthens the case for a more restrictive RFC. The documented need for unscheduled lying down, 10% pain interference, visual discomfort, and history of three hospitalizations in under a year suggest she cannot sustain full-time competitive employment. At step 5, a vocational expert would likely testify that these limitations preclude the full range of light work and may reduce her to sedentary work or less, where the Medical-Vocational Guidelines would direct a finding of disabled for a person of her age and education.

**Overall Determination**  
Based on the five-step process and the new medical evidence, the SSA is likely to find Ms. Strum disabled. The prior denials were issued without Dr. Jenkins’ opinion, which provides contemporaneous, detailed support for the severity, onset (September 8, 2023), and ongoing nature of her impairments. The combination of Crohn’s disease with documented complications and uveitis supports disability, whether through meeting a listing at step 3 or a finding at step 5 that she cannot sustain other work. I recommend accepting representation and submitting the Medical Opinion Statement with the appeal, potentially requesting a hearing before an ALJ. This evidence should materially improve her prospects.

Please let me know how you would like to proceed, including any next steps for client intake or brief preparation.

Best regards,  
[Your Name]  
Associate Attorney

**Determination: Berry’s property passes by intestacy under New York law.**

Berry executed two identical wills in 2015, one kept in his New York apartment and the other at his Hamptons vacation home. Both wills disposed of his estate by leaving one-half to son Jack and one-half to daughter Jill, with nothing to son Michael. Berry died in 2025. The executor located the New York copy but could not locate the Hamptons copy despite the facts presented.

Under New York law, when a testator executes a will in duplicate, each duplicate constitutes the full will. Revocation of one duplicate by destruction with revocatory intent revokes the entire will (see *Crossman v. Crossman*, 95 N.Y. 145, 152 (1884)). When a will that was once in the testator’s possession cannot be found after a diligent search following death, a strong presumption arises that the testator destroyed it with the intent to revoke (*In re Lewis*, 25 N.Y.3d 499 (2015); see also *Matter of Fox*, 9 N.Y.2d 400, 407–408 (1961)). This presumption applies even when duplicates exist; the destruction of one duplicate (or the inability to locate it where the testator kept it) triggers revocation of all copies.

Here, the facts indicate that Berry kept one duplicate at his Hamptons vacation home. The inability to locate it after his death raises the presumption that he revoked the will by destroying that duplicate. No evidence is provided to rebut the presumption. Therefore, neither duplicate is valid, and the 2015 wills are revoked.

Because Berry died without a valid will, his estate passes by intestate succession under EPTL § 4-1.1. With no surviving spouse mentioned, the three children—Jack, Jill, and Michael—share equally. Each takes a one-third interest in Berry’s property.

The executor should present the located New York duplicate to the Surrogate’s Court and account for the missing Hamptons duplicate. The court will then determine whether the presumption of revocation is rebutted; on the facts given, it is not.
